text.content:
Who she is

Veska Maelan, mid-fifties, Hardby-born, trade-clearing notary by profession and by family — her mother and grandmother both held the same office on Whalemarket Quay. She reads the Aerdi clearing-marks better than anyone outside the firms themselves and reads the Rel Astran private chops better than most clerks who actually work for them. She has been Caelith's Hardby contact for twelve years; their correspondence runs in cipher (a Yeomanry diplomatic variant she helped him refine) by sealed packet through a chain of trusted couriers.

Description

Compact, dark-eyed, gray hair worn short under a soft brown cap most days. Plain wool dress, no jewelry except a small silver scale-and-scroll badge at the throat (her notarial seal of office). Reading lenses on a cord around her neck. Quiet, watchful, slow-moving — the kind of professional whose stillness reads as authority.

Where she is

Above her notary office on Whalemarket Quay, two doors east of the Quay's main clearing-house. The office is a single ground-floor room with a desk, a tall standing-cabinet of ledgers and copybooks, two visitor chairs, and a brass-and-green-glass desk lamp. Her residence is upstairs — a small kitchen, a bedchamber, a bath. She lives alone; her partner of thirty years died at sea in 578.

Hours

Open by appointment second through sixth bell after dawn. She does not work in the afternoon. After the seventh bell of evening she will not open her door for anyone she did not write to first.

Approach phrase

For unannounced visitors: "A tower above three waves." Spoken at the threshold. If she is alone and free to speak, she repeats it back. If she does not repeat it, she is not alone, and the visitor should leave and try later. Do not try to push past or improvise.

Availability by travel approach

How available Veska is to the party depends on which travel approach the party chose at the end of Phase 2 (see Phase 3 Arrival → GM Brief):

  • Quiet Pursuit (best case). She is in her office, alone, fully cooperative. She has been waiting for Caelith's quiet visitors per his last cipher. The phrase opens the door. She will spend an evening with the party going through her notes and the chop-rubbings she has assembled.
  • Commercial Cover. She is alive in Hardby, cautious. She will receive the party in business hours only, with Caelith's letter of introduction visible on the desk. She will help once she has read the letter twice and concluded the party can be trusted; this is one or two visits' work.
  • Official Delegation (worst case). She has fled Hardby. Her office is shuttered; the cabinet of ledgers is gone. A former apprentice of hers, Hella Voren (young scribe, mid-twenties, works at the Quay clearing-house), can be approached privately and will pass the party Veska's notes once they speak the agreed phrase. The notes are as useful as Veska in person, but the party loses Veska's spoken context — and they have no way to ask her follow-up questions.

What she knows

Read aloud in her voice as the party walks her through the recovered evidence:

  • The crane-and-coins seal on the Vandrell escrow is Cindren & Vhal house seal, exactly as Phase 1 indicated. Standard.
  • The Hardby clearing-chop on the same escrow's countersign — the second ink mark, the one without a partner's initial — was applied by Solen Mereth, head of the Hardby branch's Bond-Loading Office. Veska knows Solen's hand the way a wine-taster knows a particular vineyard. She can show the party three other documents in her own files where Solen used the same chop and the same hand.
  • The marginal endorsement — "Hold for V., or his agent of record, against presentation" — is in a different hand, smaller, and not Solen's. It is a hand Veska has seen on three documents in the last four years, all involving payouts to one private clearing account she has never been able to trace to a name. She believes — without proof — that the hand belongs to Tarlith Vhal Sereth, the recluse senior partner at Cindren & Vhal Rel Astra. If pressed she will say the same thing the party already half-suspects: "If I am wrong, I am wrong by one office. If I am right, the man you are looking for has not signed a letter at his own desk in eleven years."
  • The tower-and-waves countermark on the clipped silver coin is, of course, Cindren & Vhal's internal clearing token, but Veska knows something else about it: that specific countermark stamp — there are seven of them, distributed across the firm's offices — is the Hardby branch's stamp, recognizable by a faint chip on the lower-right wave that Veska herself notarized as a defect in 579 CY when Solen Mereth replaced it for the firm's records and then quietly kept the old one. The clipped coin in Haskur's pack was weighed in at Hardby, by Solen's office, on Solen's defect-stamp, and that stamp officially does not exist anymore.
  • Tarsh. Veska does not know him by that name personally. She does know a man matching Sarth's description — thin, fifties, smoker's rasp — who is a freelance broker working out of the Coopered Wreck tavern on the harbor district edge. She knew him during his C&V years (he was a clerk in Solen's bond-loading office for six years) and she knows his real name: Anver Resh. He left the firm "to manage his own affairs" four years ago, which she understood at the time and understands more clearly now to mean he was given a different role outside the books.
  • Tamsin Moraven. Veska has never met her. She has, however, seen Moraven's queries cross her desk three times in the last year — Yeomanry trade-clearing requests on grain shipments to South Province that Veska's office had to clear or refuse. She refused them all on procedural grounds and forwarded copies, quietly, to Caelith. "She was very close," Veska says, "and I was not brave enough to write to her directly."

What she will not do

Veska will not break Hardby civic law. She is a notary; her credibility depends on her absolute reputation for procedural correctness. She will not forge documents, will not steal documents, will not lie under oath, will not vouch for the party's false names if asked. She will read documents the party brings her, identify hands and chops, write a brief sworn notarial opinion the party can present in any Aerdi or Yeomanry court (and this is what they need — her opinion, properly notarized, is what makes the document evidence rather than rumor), and pass them what she has from her own files.

Stat block

See Veska Maelan in the Phase 3 NPCs compendium. CR 1 (non-combatant). She will not fight. If pressed, she yields and offers her notarial seal in trade for her safety; she will reconstruct any document she has yielded once she is safe.

Who he is

Castrian Vell, late thirties, Hardby resident, poet and minor playwright. Trina sponsored his early career in Loftwick fifteen years ago, paid his first publication, attended his first reading. He has lived in Hardby for the last decade; he keeps a small townhouse on a quiet lane off Rope Walk in the Salters' Quarter, hosts a salon every third evening, and is read at three of the city's better dining rooms.

Description

Curls graying at the temples. Clean unringed hands; he is not married and never has been. Wears soft dark fabrics; an emerald-and-silver pin at the collar that is the only ornament he ever puts on. Speaks in long careful sentences he has clearly composed in his head before speaking. Smiles often; the smile is genuine half the time, and a careful imitation of itself the other half.

Where he is

Townhouse: tall stone-fronted, two stories, on a lane off Rope Walk. The door bears a small brass plate: VELL. Sitting room downstairs (small but warm, with a hearth, two settees, a narrow shelf of his own published volumes). Study off the back where he writes. Three guest bedchambers upstairs (he can lodge the entire party tightly). Kitchen run by his housekeeper, Sarro Pell, a widow in her sixties who has worked for him eleven years and notices everything about everyone.

Availability

Available to the party only on the Commercial Cover approach. On Quiet Pursuit, the party may visit him secondarily if they think to; he will receive them but cannot offer the same level of cover (he was expecting them with Trina's letter, not without). On Official Delegation, he politely refuses to be involved with an accredited delegation that the firm is watching — Trina warned him about this and he respects her judgment; he sends his regrets and a small envelope with the address of his Whalemarket Quay barber, who passes letters discreetly.

What he offers (Commercial Cover)

  • Lodging. All three guest bedchambers, hot water, breakfast, the same key the housekeeper carries.
  • The salon network. Castrian's salon every third evening. He will introduce the party as "Mistress Alvere's wool friends from the west." This gives them face-to-face access in a single evening to: a Salters' Quarter dyer who supplies Cindren & Vhal Hardby with sealing wax, a Gynarchy under-clerk who sees the harbor commission's appointment ledgers cross her desk, a retired ship's captain who can tell them which warehouses on Black Cog Lane have changed hands in the last year, and a sculptor whose current apprentice is a young woman named Ailen Moraven.
  • His city ear. Castrian knows who is sleeping with whom, who has lost money lately, who has gained money strangely, which counting house has had a clerk leave under quiet circumstances. He will share what he knows freely with the party once he trusts them, but he is careful never to write anything down.

His weakness

Castrian is more talkative when he drinks, which is most evenings. Manage his cups in his own home and he is an asset. Never let him drink in a public room with people who might be the firm's eyes, because by the third glass he will say something a clever listener can use. Sarro Pell knows this and has, for eleven years, watered his evening wine without telling him.

What he does not know

Castrian does not know about the conspiracy in any organized sense. He knows Hardby is going badly, that the harbor commission is being talked about more than usual, and that two of his salon regulars have stopped attending in the last six months — both clerks at C&V Hardby. He has not put the pieces together. He is a poet who likes to live well; he is not a spy.

He will, however, recognize the brass Hand-of-the-Duke token if the party shows it to him in private. He will go very quiet for a long moment, and then ask, "Is Caelith well?" When told yes, he will pour himself a careful single measure of brandy, drink it, and say, "What do you need from me?" After that he is fully the party's, within the limits above.

Use this page only if Phase 2's Pressure Clock segment 5 fired and Hesren Vesh was recalled from Loftwick to Hardby. If the party left Loftwick before segment 5, Hesren is still in Loftwick and unavailable.

Where he is

Lodged in a small firm-owned residence two streets from the Cindren & Vhal Hardby branch — a narrow second-floor apartment above a chandler's shop on Long Tide Street. The apartment is supervised. The chandler downstairs and the young man at the corner of Long Tide and Rope Walk both work for the firm and report comings and goings.

His situation

Hesren has been "reassigned" to a senior-clerk role with no real responsibilities. He is paid, fed, allowed to walk in the city, and not allowed to write or receive correspondence that has not passed through the office. He has guessed (correctly) that he will not be senior much longer, and (incorrectly) that the firm intends to retire him to a coastal residence rather than to a worse fate.

He is afraid. He has not said so to anyone. He has been writing a careful private journal in a small unbound notebook he keeps under the mattress. The journal is in plain Common, no cipher — Hesren has none of Caelith's tradecraft.

Approaching him

Hesren cannot be approached at the apartment without alerting the firm. He can be approached at any of three places he visits in the city without supervision:

  • The Whalemarket fishmonger row, daily before the third bell after dawn. Hesren walks the row alone every morning to buy his own breakfast. The fishmongers know him by face but not by name; he buys hot bread and a small fillet and eats them on a low wall at the south end of the row.
  • The Salters' Quarter chapel, every fifth evening. Hesren attends a small private service at a chapel of Pelor (or whatever lawful-good-leaning local deity the GM prefers), evening of the fifth day. He goes alone. He sits in the back row.
  • The Coopered Wreck tavern on the harbor district edge. Hesren does not drink there, but he passes through it on his way back from the chapel because the chapel's lane connects to Long Tide via the tavern's alley. Note that this is also Tarsh's haunt; on a fifth evening Hesren and Tarsh are in the same room without speaking, by careful design on Tarsh's part. Tarsh wants to know what Hesren is thinking.

What approaching PCs experience

If Hesren recognizes a PC from the Wick Lane satellite scene in Phase 2 — specifically a PC who treated him with respect rather than threat, and did not attempt to coerce him — he will agree to a private conversation. He will choose the place: usually the chapel, in the half-hour after the service ends, when the building is empty and the door can be watched.

If he agrees to talk, he will tell them, in short careful sentences:

  • The Hardby Bond-Loading Office under Solen Mereth has been operating with a separate set of books for at least two years. Hesren saw evidence of this in Loftwick when a transfer he was processing referenced an entry that did not exist in any ledger he could access.
  • The senior partner who personally signs off on the Bond-Loading second-set entries is Tarlith Vhal Sereth. Hesren does not know this from a document; he knows it because Solen Mereth made an offhand comment in 581 about "Old Sereth's signature" that Hesren did not understand at the time and now does.
  • Hesren has the small unbound notebook under his mattress. He will not give it to the party — losing it gets him killed in three days — but he will read aloud the four entries that matter, in private, in the chapel.
  • If the party offers to extract him to Yeomanry territory under safe-conduct, he will accept on one condition: Caelith must promise him in writing that he will not be charged with anything in the Yeomanry except as a witness. The party can offer this on Caelith's behalf; Caelith will honor it.

If the party does not extract him

Hesren is murdered roughly two weeks after the party leaves Hardby for Rel Astra. His body is found in the harbor; the official cause is suicide. The party will hear of it from Caelith's correspondence. Whether they had a chance to extract him and chose not to is a campaign-weight beat the GM should treat seriously.

If the party extracts him

Caelith houses him quietly at the Little Palace under an assumed name. He becomes Phase 4's most useful witness, willing to testify in writing to any Aerdi or Yeomanry court the party can convene against Cindren & Vhal. He is the live witness who saw the second set of books from inside the firm.

Who she is

Ailen Moraven, mid-twenties, Tamsin and Vellin Moraven's eldest daughter. Studying with a sculptor (her master, Edrin Saetar, a thin formal man in his sixties) in the Salters' Quarter for the last two years. Talented; the master keeps her on for free now, having waived the second year's tuition because her work began to sell. Has not yet been told her mother is missing — Vellin could not bring himself to write the letter; Trina sent her own letter from Loftwick if the party suggested it during the Phase 2 visit to Vellin.

Description

Tall, dark-haired (her mother's hair), pale gray eyes (her father's), strong forearms from clay and stone work. Wears a leather work-apron over a plain shirt during the day; a single dark green dress for evenings. A small silver locket at the throat — gift from Tamsin on her sixteenth birthday, holds a tiny braid of her parents' hair. She does not yet know to be afraid.

Where she is

The sculptor's workshop on Bell Street, Salters' Quarter — an old bell-foundry converted to a shared workspace. She has a curtained corner there with a cot and a small stove; she does not lodge with her master but with another apprentice family two streets over. She works long days; evenings she walks the harbor wall.

Whether she knows

The two cases that affect Phase 3:

  • If the party (or Trina) sent for her in Phase 2: she received Trina's letter four days before the party arrived in Hardby, has been packing reluctantly, and is scheduled to leave for Loftwick on the second morning of Phase 3. She does not know exactly why she has been called home; Trina's letter said only "come, and come quickly." She has guessed it is bad.
  • If no one sent for her in Phase 2: she has heard nothing from her family in five weeks. This is not unusual — Vellin writes irregularly and Tamsin's correspondence runs in irregular bursts. She has not yet thought to be alarmed. She is alone in the city without any of the warnings the party brought from Loftwick.

What she can offer the party

  • Her mother's papers. Tamsin sent Ailen a small bundle of correspondence three months ago for "safekeeping" — Ailen assumed it was sentimental. It is not. The bundle contains carbons of three of Tamsin's filed queries against C&V Hardby, two private letters Tamsin wrote and never sent (one to Caelith, one to a Hardby weighmaster named Joren Krill who turns out to be on the firm's payroll and would not have helped), and a small private list in Tamsin's hand of every grain shipment she suspected was forged in the last eighteen months. The list is the most valuable single document the party will recover in Phase 3 outside the C&V vault — it is what gives Veska what she needs to prepare a notarial opinion that names Tarlith Vhal Sereth.
  • A safe house. The sculptor's workshop on Bell Street has a back garden gate onto a service alley; the cellar holds clay-prep tools and a low cot the master used during a long commission years ago. The party can use it as an emergency lay-up spot if needed; Ailen will not ask why. Trina has written ahead about the party on the Commercial Cover or Quiet Pursuit approaches; Ailen will recognize the party's names and trust them on first meeting.

What she should not be told

If Tamsin is alive on the Quiet Pursuit or Commercial Cover paths, do not tell Ailen until Tamsin is recovered. Ailen, told her mother is in immediate danger, will go to whichever location the party names and try to help. She is competent and well-loved in the Quarter; she is also entirely untrained for combat or rescue work and will get herself killed.

If Tamsin is confirmed dead (Official Delegation path), the party must decide who tells her and how. Trina's letter, if she has it, gave Ailen the warning she needed; the actual telling is still cruel work. Castrian, if he is in play, will offer to do it — he has done this kind of telling for friends before. The party may accept his offer; some PCs may want to do it themselves. Either is right.

What she does in Phase 4 and beyond

If Ailen leaves for Loftwick safely, she becomes a quiet thread in later phases — Trina's protégée, eventually a sculptor whose work appears at the Yeomanry Council building. If she stays in Hardby and Tamsin is recovered, the family reunites in Loftwick after Phase 3 with the party as escorts. If Tamsin is dead, Ailen returns alone to Vellin and the two of them carry the loss together; the party may wish to commission a portrait or memorial from her later in the campaign.

Who he is

Anver Resh, fifties, Hardby-born, formerly a clerk in Cindren & Vhal Hardby's Bond-Loading Office under Solen Mereth (1576–1582). Left the firm "to manage his own affairs" in spring 1582 and has, since then, operated as a freelance broker — hires couriers, thieves, watch officers, and assassins on commission for clients who do not want to issue contracts in their own names. The firm is one of his clients. Among other people he hires from, he is known by the working name Tarsh — a smoker's nickname from his C&V days that stuck.

Description

Thin, fifties, balding with a fringe of dark hair worn long. A long pale face. Yellow stains on the second and third fingers of his right hand from cheap pipe-leaf. A smoker's rasp in the voice. Plain dark wool, a heavy wool coat in winter, no jewelry. A small leather wallet inside his coat with a notary's stylus and a smaller knife. He looks exactly like what he is: a clerk who left the office and never quite stopped clerking.

Where he is

His official residence is two rooms above a chandler's shop on Long Tide Street, but he is rarely there. He spends most evenings at the Coopered Wreck tavern on the harbor district edge — a deep, low-ceilinged sailors' room with three back booths used for the kind of business that does not happen at counters. He takes the leftmost back booth as his by long custom; the proprietor, a one-eyed retired bosun named Olfard Trent, knows to leave him alone with whoever sits down across from him.

Resh keeps no office, no clerk, no records of any kind he writes down himself. His memory is exceptional. His verbal contracts are honored because he honors them and because the alternative is well-understood among Hardby's freelance contractors.

What he does

For Cindren & Vhal Hardby's compromised side of the business: he hires Merev Sarth and other thieves; arranges quiet contracts with city watch officers (intelligence, not violence); coordinates with the firm's loading-bond clerks on what shipments need to be "present in the docks" on what days; manages the Hardby end of the courier network that moves the conspiracy's correspondence between Hardby, Rel Astra, and (according to one of Sarth's smoke-heavy days) one further-east port Resh has refused to name even to himself.

For other clients: small contracts. Merchant rivals, marital matters, missing persons, the occasional disposal of an inconvenient debt-record. He is not picky about clients but he is picky about contracts — he refuses any commission that would expose his C&V relationship.

His one rule

Resh does not personally kill, has not personally killed, will not personally kill. He hires it done. This is not a moral position; it is a survival strategy. A broker who gets blood on his own hands is a broker who can be evidence. He has held to this rule for sixteen years.

Availability by travel approach

  • Quiet Pursuit (best case). Resh is in his usual booth at the Coopered Wreck most evenings between the seventh and tenth bells. The party can confront him there. He is unprepared, has no bodyguards on retainer this week, and will give names if cornered with appropriate pressure.
  • Commercial Cover. Resh is at the Coopered Wreck about every other evening. The other evenings he is at his apartment with a single hired bodyguard outside the door. He has been told, by the firm, to be careful but has not been told what to be careful of. He can still be confronted in person; he is more guarded but not yet braced.
  • Official Delegation (worst case). Resh has been moved upcountry to a small farm outside Hardby's jurisdiction. The party never confronts him in person on this approach. They identify him through paper trails (Veska's records, the C&V vault if they breach it, Hesren's notebook if they have him). Naming him publicly is enough to expose him; he loses Hardby and goes to ground further east.

What he gives up if cornered

Resh is a survivor, not a fanatic — same as Sarth. If the party gets him in a private room, has clear leverage (the smoking-gun document from Veska, or testimony from Hesren, or simply outnumbers him with witnesses present), he yields what he knows in exchange for a one-way passport out of Hardby and a guarantee that his name does not appear in the eventual indictment. He will:

  • Confirm Solen Mereth. Solen runs the second-set of books out of the Hardby branch, has done since 1580. Resh worked under him.
  • Name Tarlith Vhal Sereth as the senior partner who signs off. Resh has never met Sereth in person — no one outside the inner senior partners has, in eleven years — but he knows Sereth's seal and has carried two sealed packets to Solen on Sereth's instruction. He can describe the seal in detail (an unfamiliar variant of the firm's house chop, with a small additional mark Resh always assumed was a partner's personal sign).
  • Reveal where Tamsin Moraven is held. If she is still in Hardby (Quiet Pursuit) — the warehouse on Black Cog Lane, owned through the shell company "Greenwall & Mast." If she has been moved (Commercial Cover) — the Whitemoor Estate, two days east on the coast road. If she is dead (Official Delegation, but the party still corners him later through paper) — he confirms the harbor as her resting place and confirms it was Solen who ordered it.
  • Refuse to name his Rel Astra contact. Even with leverage, Resh will not name his counterpart in Rel Astra. "That man," he will say, "is not someone I will trade for a passport. Take what I have given you and go." The Rel Astra contact is a Phase 4 thread.

What he refuses to do under any circumstance

Resh will not testify in any court — Hardby, Yeomanry, or Aerdi. He will sign nothing. The party can use what he tells them as investigative leads but cannot use his name as a witness. "I am giving you what is in my head," he says. "You will not also have what is in my hand."

If the party kills him

Strongly discouraged. Without Resh alive (and conditionally cooperative), the party loses the Tamsin location lead on Quiet Pursuit (they will find her late, with consequences) and loses the verbal corroboration of Solen and Sereth. The smoking-gun documents from Veska + the C&V vault are still enough to prosecute, but the operational picture for Phase 4 is much weaker. If the party kills him, the firm assumes the party did so under contract — i.e., that someone hired the killing — and Phase 4 begins under the assumption that a third party is in play, which the GM may use as fertile uncertainty.

Stat block

See Anver "Tarsh" Resh in the Phase 3 NPCs compendium. CR 6, but he will not fight if he can avoid it. If he is at the Coopered Wreck and a fight breaks out, his bodyguard (if present) and a paid pair of harbor-district enforcers nearby will defend him while he exits through Olfard's back room.

pages.name: Zoria Weis — Caelith's Hardby Asset (Use With Care)

text.content:
Zoria Weis is named in Caelith's second letter (see Arrival → Caelith's Continuing Letters). She is a Gynarchy under-clerk who owes Caelith a favor and may be useful to the party. She is NOT a friend; she is a debt being called.

Who she is

Zoria Weis, late thirties, an under-clerk in the Gynarchy's Office of Mercantile Records — the civic clearing-house that registers every commercial transaction in Hardby that the firms cannot keep entirely off the official books. She has been an under-clerk for fourteen years and will be an under-clerk for fourteen more, by both temperament and design. Caelith helped her brother out of a Yeomanry trade dispute in 577 in a way that meant her brother kept his shop; she has owed Caelith a quiet debt since.

Description

Plain, careful, neither tall nor short, brown hair pinned up under a small white cap. Wears the Gynarchy under-clerk's grey-and-cream half-livery during business hours; plain wool dresses in her own time. Speaks in dry careful sentences. Smiles rarely. Does not laugh in public.

Approach phrase

The party knows of her only through Caelith's second letter. He gives the phrase: "The autumn ledger." She will respond, if free to: "Has been settled." If she does not respond, she is not free; do not push.

Where she is

The Gynarchy's Office of Mercantile Records, on the Council steps in the Old Quarter. She works at the southwest desk, third in from the door. After hours she lives alone in a small flat above a tea-house in the Salters' Quarter; she walks the long way home past the harbor wall every night.

What she will do

  • Read the harbor commission's appointment ledger to the party. The Office of Mercantile Records is not the harbor commission, but its scribes copy commission appointments into a parallel registry as a matter of civic record. Zoria can show the party who currently holds each of the seven commission seats, who appointed them, and what the current vacancies are. This is the document the party needs to begin understanding what "harbor before throne" operationally means.
  • Pass small messages. Zoria can carry a sealed letter to or from anyone in the city under the cover of normal Gynarchy business. She will not carry anything she has read. She will not carry anything to or from the firm.
  • Vouch for the party once — and only once — to a single Gynarchy official of the party's choosing. She will not vouch to a council member; she has no standing for that. She will vouch to another under-clerk, or to a watch sergeant of her acquaintance.

What she will not do

Zoria will not betray the Gynarchy. If the party asks her to do something that would compromise the Gynarchy's institutional integrity, she refuses, even if it would help Caelith. She is not Caelith's; she is the Gynarchy's, and her debt to Caelith is paid in services that are useful to the party but compatible with her primary loyalty.

The thing the party should know about her

Zoria reports to the Gynarchy. Anything she sees, the Gynarchy eventually sees, in her own writing, in the under-clerks' weekly summary. She will not lie to her superiors. If the party tells her something they do not want eventually known to the Gynarchy, they should not tell it to her. Caelith's letter says this in fewer words: "Do not say anything to her you would not want eventually said to the Gynarchy proper."

Used carefully, she is the party's bridge to Hardby's institutional record. Used carelessly, she is the way the firm finds out the party is in town.
